title: These 3 Stocks Were Once Hot Buys. Now They're Down More Than 50% From Their Highs. Can They Bounce Back?
author: David Jagielski, Cpa
description: Three previously popular tech stocks—ServiceNow, MicroStrategy, and Oracle—have experienced significant declines this year. ServiceNow (down 36%) faces AI disruption concerns but shows strong fundamentals; MicroStrategy (down 44%) is heavily dependent on Bitcoin volatility; Oracle (down 25%) struggles with rising debt loads and interest expenses despite strong cloud growth projections.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Stock down 36% YTD and 50% from highs due to AI disruption fears, but strong revenue growth (22%), beat guidance, and raised outlook suggest fundamentals remain solid. Trading at reasonable 24x forward earnings multiple indicates potential buying opportunity despite risks.

Down 25% YTD and 60% from highs. Despite strong cloud growth projections (57%+), rising debt load ($43B raised last year, $40B projected this year) and $1.4B quarterly interest expense create investor concerns. Author recommends steering clear due to debt risks and potential interest rate increases.

Down 25% YTD and 60% from highs. Despite strong cloud growth projections (57%+), rising debt load ($43B raised last year, $40B projected this year) and $1.4B quarterly interest expense create investor concerns. Author recommends steering clear due to debt risks and potential interest rate increases.

title: Up 275% This Year: Is It Too Late to Buy Bloom Energy Stock?
author: Manali Pradhan, Cfa
description: Bloom Energy stock has surged 275% in 2026 as AI data centers drive demand for its fuel cell power systems. Major customers like Oracle and partnerships with Brookfield Asset Management validate the technology, with strong Q1 financials showing 130% revenue growth. However, the stock's rich 38x sales valuation, customer concentration risk, and sharp rally suggest investors should build positions gradually rather than buy aggressively.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Strong fundamentals with 130% revenue growth, major customer contracts (Oracle, Brookfield), and improving margins support the business. However, the 275% rally has created a rich 38x sales valuation, and significant customer concentration (50% from two customers) poses risks. The article suggests the easy gains may be behind, warranting a cautious approach.

Oracle's commitment to 1.2-2.8 gigawatts of Bloom fuel cell capacity and ability to deploy systems in 55 days demonstrates strong validation of the technology and Oracle's strategic positioning in AI infrastructure.

Oracle's commitment to 1.2-2.8 gigawatts of Bloom fuel cell capacity and ability to deploy systems in 55 days demonstrates strong validation of the technology and Oracle's strategic positioning in AI infrastructure.

Brookfield's planned $5 billion investment in deploying Bloom's technology for AI infrastructure shows confidence in the fuel cell solution and positions the company as a major player in AI infrastructure development.
AEP's agreement to purchase up to 1 gigawatt of Bloom fuel cells, starting with a 100-megawatt order, demonstrates utility company adoption of the technology for AI infrastructure needs.

title: OpenAI Just Signaled It Might Delay Its IPO. AI Stocks Are Tumbling. Is the Bubble Bursting?
author: Daniel Sparks
description: Reports that OpenAI is considering delaying its IPO until 2027 to achieve a $1 trillion valuation sparked a selloff in AI and chip stocks. OpenAI's $600 billion computing capacity commitments underpin much of the AI industry's expected demand, so hesitation to go public raises questions about the durability of AI spending. However, signed deals and strong recent chip demand suggest the AI build-out still has momentum, though valuations leave little room for error if spending slows.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Stock slipped ~1.5% on IPO delay news. OpenAI's hesitation to raise capital threatens the company's largest customer's spending plans. However, recent 85% YoY revenue growth and 92% data center growth suggest underlying demand remains strong, tempering the negative outlook.
Stock fell further than Nvidia on the news. AMD has a $6 billion chip deal with OpenAI, making it vulnerable to any slowdown in OpenAI's spending commitments.
Stock declined significantly (-3.39%). Broadcom is developing custom chips with OpenAI and is exposed to risks from delayed capital deployment and IPO uncertainty.
Stock fell -2.55%. Oracle has a reported $300 billion cloud computing deal with OpenAI, making it directly exposed to any reduction in OpenAI's spending pace.
Stock fell -2.55%. Oracle has a reported $300 billion cloud computing deal with OpenAI, making it directly exposed to any reduction in OpenAI's spending pace.

Mentioned as a cautionary example of a recent mega-IPO that debuted strongly but has since declined. Used as a reference point for why OpenAI advisors recommended waiting for better market conditions.

title: Stock Market Today, June 25: Microsoft Falls as AI Spending Concerns Pressure Cloud Margin Outlook
author: Eric Trie
description: Microsoft stock fell 3.45% to $352.83 after Stifel cut its price target from $415 to $400, citing concerns that margin expectations may be unrealistic as the company increases AI infrastructure investment. While Azure growth continues to support the stock, investors are assessing whether cloud and AI product demand can offset rising operational costs. The broader tech sector also weakened, with Oracle and Alphabet declining.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Stock declined 3.45% following Stifel's price target reduction and investor concerns about unrealistic margin expectations amid increased AI infrastructure spending. Despite Azure growth support, profitability pressures are weighing on the stock.
Stock fell 3.10% as part of broader large-cap tech weakness, indicating sector-wide concerns about cloud and AI-related cost pressures.
Stock fell 3.10% as part of broader large-cap tech weakness, indicating sector-wide concerns about cloud and AI-related cost pressures.
Stock declined 0.82% as part of the broader technology sector weakness affecting large-cap tech companies.

title: SpaceX (SPCX) Is Down 31% From Its High and Is Now Borrowing $25 Billion. Should Investors Be Worried?
author: Selena Maranjian
description: SpaceX stock has fallen 31% from its $225 peak to $154 following its June IPO, and the company is now borrowing $25 billion despite raising $85.7 billion. The article raises concerns about SpaceX's steep $2 trillion valuation (price-to-sales ratio of 77), current unprofitability, heavy AI spending, and capital-intensive operations. The author recommends waiting for a much lower price before investing, citing the speculative nature of the investment and leadership concerns.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Stock down 31% from IPO high; company is unprofitable with steep valuation (P/S ratio of 77); borrowing $25 billion despite recent IPO; heavy AI spending with uncertain returns; capital-intensive business model; author recommends waiting for lower prices due to speculative risk.

Mentioned as comparison point for valuation metrics; has P/S ratio of 20 (lower than SpaceX's 77); positioned as AI powerhouse but used only for relative valuation context, not as investment recommendation.
Mentioned as another Elon Musk company; not the focus of analysis; included in disclosure of Motley Fool positions but no specific sentiment expressed in article.
Used as valuation comparison; SpaceX's $2 trillion market cap exceeds Oracle's despite SpaceX having only $19 billion in revenue versus Oracle's $64 billion; mentioned for context only.
